key_insight: An optimization-based phase-field calibration can embed target sharp-interface solute-trapping and solute-drag response functions, enabling quantitative rapid-solidification modeling with large diffuse interfaces.
engineering_relevance: This gives alloy designers and process modelers a practical way to predict microsegregation and morphology changes under rapid solidification using physically tuned interface kinetics.
method: Treat interfacial diffusivity interpolation as a tunable response-matching parameter.
Calibrate 1D steady-state phase-field solutions to continuous-growth-model targets for velocity-dependent trapping, drag, and liquidus response.
Extend the formulation to multicomponent dilute alloys with independently specified partition coefficients and liquidus slopes.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports that the calibrated model reproduces prescribed response functions in dilute Al-Cu and that 2D directional-solidification simulations capture morphology transitions as solute drag increases.
watch_item: The approach depends on accurate target response functions and calibration in simplified 1D steady-state settings, so transferability to complex transient experiments may need further validation.
paper_title: Response-function-optimized phase field modeling of solute trapping and solute drag in rapid alloy solidification
